coredata.dc:title: A novel ternary TiO2/CQDs/BiOX (X = Cl, Br, I) heterostructure as photocatalyst for water purification under solar irradiation
coredata.dc:creator: Qu, Xiaofei
Liu, Meihua
Yang, Junying
Wang, Chunqi
Meng, Huihui
Li, Shenggang
Shi, Liang
Du, Fanglin
coredata.dc:description: A series of novel binary TiO2/BiOX (X = Cl, Br, I) and ternary TiO2/CQDs/BiOBr hybrid photocatalysts were prepared through a facile hydrothermal method. TiO2/BiOX heterostructures were fabricated by varying the halogen ions. The photocatalysts were characterized by X-ray diffraction, scanning electron microscopy, transmission electron microscopy, UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. Among the binary TiO2/BiOX, TiO2/BiOBr showed the most preferable photocatalytic activities due to suitable band gap alignment and photo stability, reaching almost 80% MO degradation during 180 min irradiation under simulated sunlight. Moreover, the effects of synthesis temperature, Ti/Bi ratio on the composition and structure were investigated, the results indicated TiO2/BiOBr with Bi/Ti = 10% prepared at 120 °C possessed the best photocatalytic performance. Carbon quantum dots were introduced to accelerate electrons transfer at interface or prevent charges recombination on the surface, resulting a much better photocatalytic performance for TiO2/CQDs/BiOBr hybrid photocatalysts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bismuth oxyhalide
Carbon quantum dots
Photocatalytic performance
Water purification

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of TiO2/BiOX (X = Cl, Br, I) composites
Effects of halogen (X = Cl, Br, I) on the composition, morphology and optical properties of TiO2-BiOX composites
Effects of hydrothermal temperature and Bi/Ti on the composition, morphology and optical properties ofTiO2/BiOBr composites
Effects of Carbon quantum dots loading on the composition, morphology and optical properties ofTiO2-BiOBr composites
Possible mechanism of enhanced photocatalytic performance by ternary TiO2/CQDs/BiOBr composites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of (a) bare TiO2 (b) TiO2/BiOCl (c) TiO2/BiOBr (d) and TiO2/BiOI.
SEM images of (a) TiO2/BiOCl (b) TiO2/BiOBr and (c) TiO2/BiOI.
(a) UV–Vis diffuse-reflectance spectra of TiO2 and TiO2/BiOX (X = Cl, Br, I). (b) Photocatalytic performance of the samples towards MO degradation;.
XRD patterns of TiO2 and TiO2/BiOBr prepared at different temperature (40–160 °C).
SEM images of the TiO2/BiOBr prepared at different temperature: (a) 40 °C (b) 60 °C (c) 120 °C (d) 160 °C.
XRD patterns of TiO2 and TiO2/BiOBr with different Bi/Ti ratios.
Photocatalytic degradation plots of MO by (a) TiO2/BiOBr prepared at different temperatures (b) TiO2/BiOBr with different Bi/Ti ratios.
XRD patterns for (a) TiO2 (b) TiO2/CQDs (c) TiO2/BiOBr. (d) TiO2/CQDs/BiOBr.
(a) XPS spectrum of TiO2/CQDs, TiO2/BiOBr, and TiO2/CQDs/BiOBr (b)-(f) High-resolution XPS spectra of Br, Bi, C, Ti, and O.
(a) TEM and SAED analysis of TiO2/CQDs/BiOBr (b) High-resolution TEM image of TiO2/CQDs/BiOBr.
N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm of (a) TiO2 (b) TiO2/BiOBr and (c) TiO2/CQDs/BiOBr.
(a) UV–Vis diffuse-reflectance spectra of TiO2, TiO2/CQDs, TiO2/BiOBr, and TiO2/CQDs/BiOBr (b) Photocatalytic activity of MO by TiO2, TiO2/CQDs, TiO2/BiOBr, and TiO2/CQDs/BiOBr.
Illustration of the synthetic process of TiO2/CQDs/BiOBr.
Illustration of enhanced MO photocatalytic degradation by TiO2/BiOX (X = Cl, Br, I) under simulated sunlight.
Illustration of TiO2/BiOBr heterostructures formation via hydrothermal process.
Illustration of synergistic mechanism in ternary TiO2/CQDs/BiOBr heterostructures.

head.ce:title: A novel ternary TiO2/CQDs/BiOX (X = Cl, Br, I) heterostructure as photocatalyst for water purification under solar irradiation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Zhengzhou Road 53, Qingdao 266042, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A series of novel binary TiO2/BiOX (X = Cl, Br, I) and ternary TiO2/CQDs/BiOBr hybrid photocatalysts were prepared through a facile hydrothermal method. TiO2/BiOX heterostructures were fabricated by varying the halogen ions. The photocatalysts were characterized by X-ray diffraction, scanning electron microscopy, transmission electron microscopy, UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. Among the binary TiO2/BiOX, TiO2/BiOBr showed the most preferable photocatalytic activities due to suitable band gap alignment and photo stability, reaching almost 80% MO degradation during 180 min irradiation under simulated sunlight. Moreover, the effects of synthesis temperature, Ti/Bi ratio on the composition and structure were investigated, the results indicated TiO2/BiOBr with Bi/Ti = 10% prepared at 120 °C possessed the best photocatalytic performance. Carbon quantum dots were introduced to accelerate electrons transfer at interface or prevent charges recombination on the surface, resulting a much better photocatalytic performance for TiO2/CQDs/BiOBr hybrid photocatalysts.

The enhanced photocatalytic mechanism of TiO2/CQDs/BiOBr was proposed in above image.
fx1

ce:section.ce:para: Environmental pollution, especially water contamination, has been one of the most tremendous crisis for human beings with the rapid development of industry [1]. As one of the promising technologies to solve the problem effectively, semiconductor photocatalysis has attracted extensive attention due to excellent performance and good stability [2,3]. Since Frank et al.[3] discovered TiO2 photoanodes possessed the photocatalytic degradation towards polychlorobiphenyls in 1976. Among the traditional semiconductor photocatalysts, titanium dioxide (TiO2) has been attracted great attention [4–6], but the wide band gap (3.2 eV) and rapid recombination of photo-induced charges limit its light absorption and efficiency [7]. To date, continuous attempts have been made to improve photo-response and charge separation efficiency, including transitional metal ions doping, non-metal ions doping, noble metals loading and semiconductors coupling [8–10].

Bismuth oxyhalides (BiOX (X = Cl, Br, I)) are promising photocatalysts and have attracted much attention in recent years because of good chemical stability, environmental-friendly qualities and suitable band gap [11–13]. Many researches have been carried out to study the coupled photocatalysts TiO2/BiOX (X = Cl, Br, I) up to now, as BiOX could broaden the light response and enhance charges separation. For instance, TiO2/BiOCl composites showed excellent photocatalytic performance towards gaseous benzene degradation, achieving fully degradation of 150 ppm benzene within 80 min [14]. TiO2/BiOBr heterostructures were fabricated via a facile one-pot solvothermal approach, and their photocatalytic performance surpassed TiO2 under visible light irradiation, attributing to the open mesoporous structure and synergistic effect between TiO2 and BiOBr [15]. BiOI-sensitized TiO2 films were fabricated via simple room-temperature sequential ionic layer adsorption and reaction (SILAR) method, showing the excellent photocatalytic activity for the degradation of Rhodamine B and 4-Chlorophenol under visible-light irradiation [16]. In addition, various morphologies of TiO2/BiOX and their photocatalytic performance were also studied, such as flower-like of TiO2/BiOCl spheres [17], flake-structural TiO2/BiOBr [18] and so on.

Novel carbon materials, including carbon nanotube, carbon quantum dot and graphene, are extensively studied due to chemical inertness, nontoxicity, and excellent biocompatibility [19–22]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), because of their excellent electron transfer ability, have been applied in fabrication of photocatalytic systems such as BiOBr/BiOCl/CQDs, g-C3N4/CQDs, and TiO2/CQDs [23–25]. However, there have been few reports about the ternary TiO2/CQDs/BiOBr system applied in photocatalytic treatment of water pollution up to now.

In this work, we presented a simple method for the preparation of TiO2/BiOX (Cl, Br, I) composites. The effects of halogen on the composition, morphology and optical properties of TiO2/BiOX were systematically investigated. Moreover, TiO2/CQDs/BiOBr was also prepared via one-step hydrothermal process to further investigate the effects of CQDs on TiO2/BiOBr composites. The ternary TiO2/CQDs/BiOBr heterostructures displayed outstanding photocatalytic property in the degradation of methyl orange under simulated sunlight and the possible mechanism for the enhanced water-soluble contaminants removal was also proposed.

Titanium tetraisopropoxide (TTIP, 97%) and bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (AR, 99.0%) were purchased from Macklin Inc. Potassium nitrate (KNO3, 99%), potassium chloride (KCl, 99.5%), potassium bromide (KBr, 99%), potassium iodide (KI, 99%), ethylene glycol (EG, 99%) and absolute ethanol were all analytical grade and purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.

In a typical procedure, 0.8 mL of KNO3 solution (0.1 mol/L) was dissolved into 200 mL of ethanol. Then, 4.4 mL of TTIP was added to the above solution, and the solution was stirred continuously until white precipitate generated. The suspension was aged for 12 h in static condition, and the white paste was collected by centrifuging and washing with deionized water and ethanol several times, respectively. After the white paste was dried at 60 °C for 12 h, the obtained powders were grounded and calcined at 450 °C for 3 h, resulting TiO2 microspheres.

Carbon quantum dots were prepared according to the reference [26]. Citric acid anhydrous (960.6 mg) was dissolved in deionized water (20 mL) In the case of continuous magnetic stirring, diethylenetriamine (2710 μL) was slowly added into the above solution and kept continuously stirring. Then the solution was transferred to Teflon-lined autoclave for 6 h at 120 °C, and cooled to room temperature. The product was purified and precipitated with acetone, and CQDs were obtained eventually after drying at 60 °C

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of TiO2/BiOX (X = Cl, Br, I) composites
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly, TiO2 powders (258 mg) obtained above were dispersed in 10 mL of ethylene glycol, forming a white suspension. Afterwards, a Bi(NO3)3 solution prepared by dissolving 156.9 mg of Bi(NO3)3 in 7 mL of ethylene glycol, was dropped into the TiO2 suspension in 15 min. Then another EG solution (6 mL) contained 38.5 mg of KBr was dropped into the previous mixture gradually. After stirring for 60 min, a yellow solution was transferred into the Teflon-lined autoclave and kept for 3 h at different temperature (40 °C, 80 °C, 120 °C, and 160 °C). The resulting precipitates were collected, washed with ethanol and deionized water sufficiently, and dried. The powders were labeled as TiO2/BiOBr(x), where x represents hydrothermal temperature. A series of TiO2/BiOBr composites with different Bi/Ti molar ratio were synthesized at 120 °C, and labeled as TiO2/BiOBr (120)-y where y represents Bi/Ti molar ratio.
In addition, TiO2/BiOCl (120) and TiO2/BiOI (120) composites were also fabricated via the similar procedure using KCl and KI solutions, respectively.

The synthetic process of TiO2/CQDs/BiOBr is illustrated in Scheme 1. CQDs (300 mg) and TiO2 powders (258 mg) obtained above were dispersed in 10 mL of EG, forming a homogeneous mixture. Afterwards, the identical amount of Bi(NO3)3 and KBr solution as the former procedure were added into the above mixture dropwise, respectively. Then the mixture was kept in static at 40 °C for 3 h, following by stirring for 1 h at room temperature. Finally, the TiO2/CQDs/BiOBr composites were collected by washing with deionized water and ethanol, then drying in an oven.

The crystalline structure was obtained from the XRD patterns using an X-ray diffraction diffractometer (D-MAX2500, Rigaku Corporation, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed in an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XSAM800, Kratos Corporation, UK). The morphology of the synthesized catalyst was analyzed using field emission scanning electron microscope (JSM-6700F, JEOL Ltd., Japan) and field emission transmission electron microscope (JEM-2100, JEOL Ltd., Japan). The UV–Vis diffuse reflectance was recorded on a spectrophotometer (Cary500, Varian Corporation, USA). Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms were measured at 77 K using a surface area and pore size analyzer (NOVA 1000e, Quantachrome Instruments), and the Brunauer- Emmett- Teller (BET) method was applied to evaluate the specific surface area.

The photocatalytic performances of the samples were evaluated by degrading methyl orange (MO) under simulated sunlight using a photochemical reactor equipped with a 500 W Xe lamp. Experiments were carried out at room temperature with a circulating water system to avoid thermal-catalytic effects, and the illumination intensity was controlled and measured between 49,000 and 65,000 lx (approximately between 72 and 96 W/m−2). In a typical experiment, 20 mg of as-prepared catalysts were added into 70 mL MO solution (5 mg/L) and the mixture was stirred violently. Prior to irradiation, the suspension was placed in dark for 30 min to reach an adsorption-desorption equilibrium. During every 30 min irradiation intervals, 4 mL aliquot of the mixture was sampled and centrifuged (8000 rpm, 7 min) to remove the photocatalyst.

The photocatalytic degradation efficiency of the samples was calculated by C/C0, where C0 was the initial concentration of methyl orange, and C was the concentration at the sampling point. The MO concentrations were quantified by the absorption value at wavelength of about 450 nm, according to the Lambert-Beer law (A=k·C·l).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of halogen (X = Cl, Br, I) on the composition, morphology and optical properties of TiO2-BiOX composites
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 presented the powder XRD patterns of TiO2 and TiO2/BiOX (X = Cl, Br, I). It was noted that the predominant phase for all the composites matched with anatase TiO2 (JCPDS#84–1285) quite well. From the pattern of TiO2/BiOCl (Fig. 1 (b)), the peaks at 32.5°, 33.3° and 47.2° besides the ones from TiO2, were denoted the formation of tetragonal BiOCl (JCPDS#73-2060). In the case of TiO2/BiOBr (Fig. 1 (c)), the peaks with 2θ values at 25.3°, 32.2°, 32.5° and 46.3° could be assigned to (011), (012), (110) and (020) crystal planes of BiOBr (JCPDS#73-2061), respectively. As seen from the XRD pattern of TiO2/BiOI (Fig. 1 (d)), the major crystal planes (012), (110), and (020) were found at 24.8°, 26.1° and 45.5°, respectively, demonstrating the existence of the tetragonal BiOI (JCPDS#73-2062). Moreover, none diffraction peak shift was observed, neither the peaks with affiliations after the BiOX modifications.

The external morphology of TiO2/BiOX (X = Cl, Br, I) prepared at 120 °C for 3 h were characterized using SEM, as displayed in Fig. 2. The overall observation exhibited a spherical TiO2 particles with diameter about 300–400 nm, and flake-like BiOX deposited on the surface of TiO2 spheres. It worth to be mentioned that the amount of BiOX nanoflakes varied as different bonding capability between TiO2 and BiOX in the order of Cl, Br and I, which agreed with previous report [27]. In addition, EDS data shown in Fig. S1 , demonstrated the existence of Ti, Bi, O, and X elements, and further proved that BiOX components could successfully attach to the as-prepared TiO2 spheres through one-step hydrothermal process.

Fig. 3 (a) showed the UV–Vis diffuse-reflectance spectra of TiO2 and TiO2/BiOX composites. It could be seen that the absorption onset of TiO2/BiOBr and TiO2/BiOI showed a red-shift by about 10 nm and 40 nm compared to bare TiO2, respectively, while there was no obvious wavelength-shift for TiO2/BiOCl. Fig. 3 (b) showed the photocatalytic degradation plots of MO by bare TiO2 and TiO2/BiOX under simulated sunlight. For TiO2, the decomposition for MO could be neglected after 180 min irradiation, while the degradation efficiency for the TiO2/BiOX improved obviously. The percentage of MO degradation for TiO2/BiOCl, TiO2/BiOBr and TiO2/BiOI were 33%, 80% and 72%, respectively, suggesting that TiO2/BiOBr and TiO2/BiOI were more efficient under simulated sunlight irradiation.

Based on the optical properties and photocatalytic performance of the TiO2 and TiO2/BiOX catalysts, the possible mechanism for azo dye degradation under the simulated sunlight could be proposed in Scheme 2. As wide band gap (Eg) semiconductors, TiO2 (3.2 eV) and BiOCl (2.84 eV) possessed almost no light harvesting capability under simulated sunlight, leading to extremely poor photocatalytic activity for TiO2, while TiO2/BiOCl showed slightly improvement as enhancement of charges separation by p-n heterojunctions. However, the BiOBr (2.60 eV) and BiOI (1.69 eV) can absorb visible light to achieve much more excited electrons than BiOCl [28,29]. Moreover, when the n-type TiO2 and p-type BiOX are coupled, a build-in electric field usually occurs as the Fermi levels of the two semiconductors tend to reach equilibrium. Thus, the electrons in the conduction band of BiOX easily transfer to that of TiO2, leaving holes remaining in the valence band of BiOX, and an efficient interfacial charge separation can be achieved [30], as illustrated in Scheme 2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of hydrothermal temperature and Bi/Ti on the composition, morphology and optical properties ofTiO2/BiOBr composites
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 presented the powder X-ray diffraction patterns of the TiO2/BiOBr composites synthesized under different temperatures. Compared with that of TiO2 (2θ = 25.2°), the intensity of (012) and (112) diffraction peaks from BiOBr showed an obvious decreasing trend with the increasing of hydrothermal temperature (40–160 °C), which indicated the amount of BiOBr synthesized diminished as temperature elevated.

Fig. 5 exhibited the SEM images of TiO2/BiOBr samples prepared at different temperature. The synthesized TiO2/BiOBr composites were consisted of TiO2 spheres with an average size of about 350 nm in diameter and BiOBr nanoflakes distributed on the spherical surface, as shown in Fig. 5 (a). With temperature went up, a decreasing amount of BiOBr nanoflakes were found obviously, which could be inferred from the compositional results (Fig. 4) as well. This phenomenon may be described according to the illustration of BiOBr nucleation process, which was showed in Scheme 3. At 40 °C, the activity between Bi3+, Br- species and TiO2 was low, resulting in a uniform nuclei distribution on TiO2 spheres. With the time prolonged, more Bi3+ and Br- ions would prefer to grow on the BiOBr nuclei preformed, leading to the nanoflakes growth as intrinsic predominated characteristics of BiOBr crystalline. At higher temperature (160 °C), an enhanced collision probability between the Bi3+, Br- species and TiO2 would be responsible for a rapid and homogeneous distribution of BiOBr nuclei on TiO2 spheres. Due to the given amount of Bi3+, Br- species supply for every BiOBr site and the spatial restriction between neighboring BiOBr sites among crystal growth, a uniform layer of BiOBr occurred on the TiO2 surface.

Fig. 6 showed the XRD patterns of TiO2/BiOBr with different BiOBr contents, the main diffraction peaks of TiO2 could be observed from the patterns of as-prepared TiO2/BiOBr. The diffraction intensity of the BiOBr elevated with the increasing of BiOBr contents, indicating the crystallinity and the amount of BiOBr nanoflakes were raised.

Fig. S2 (a) indicated that the absorption edges of TiO2/BiOBr composites were about 420–450 nm with continuous red-shifts as the synthesis temperature increasing from 40 °C to 120 °C. Similarly, Fig. S2 (b) exhibited the UV–Vis absorption of TiO2/BiOBr composites with different content of BiOBr suggesting the absorption edges of TiO2/BiOBr located at about 425 nm, 455 nm, and 440 nm, respectively, with the increase of BiOBr contents.

In order to investigate the effects of synthesis temperature on photocatalytic performance, photocatalytic degradation of MO by TiO2/BiOBr synthesized at different hydrothermal temperature was conducted under simulated sunlight. As exhibited in Fig. 7 (a), TiO2/BiOBr (120) showed the highest photocatalytic activity with ~90% MO degraded after 180 min irradiation. However, TiO2/BiOBr (160) hardly possessed preferable activity probably because of the rapid crystal growth of BiOBr, leading to a simple mechanical mixture rather than heterojunctions. Fig. 7 (b) showed the photocatalytic decomposition of MO over TiO2/BiOBr synthesized at 120 °C with the different BiOBr content under simulated sunlight. Obviously, the photocatalytic activities of the samples decreased in the order as follows: TiO2/BiOBr-10%> TiO2/BiOBr-20%> TiO2/BiOBr-5%> TiO2, indicating that TiO2/BiOBr with a moderate BiOBr content was benefit to the photocatalytic performance, while excessive loading of BiOBr on TiO2 spheres decreased the degradation activity. It could be speculated that a higher dispersion of BiOBr components on the surface of TiO2 microspheres can improve light harvesting, leading to better photocatalytic activities. However, excessive loading of BiOBr may decrease the number of active sites as BiOBr possessed fewer surface area than that of TiO2 apparently.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of Carbon quantum dots loading on the composition, morphology and optical properties ofTiO2-BiOBr composites
ce:section.ce:para: TiO2/CQDs/BiOBr composites were fabricated by mixing as-prepared TiO2 spheres and CQDs together in the one-step hydrothermal process. The XRD patterns of TiO2, TiO2/CQDs, TiO2/BiOBr and TiO2/CQDs/BiOBr composites were shown in Fig. 8. The TiO2/CQDs/BiOBr had a broad peak centered at 28°, which was attributed to the highly disordered carbon atoms [31].

In order to verify the chemical state of induced CQDs, XPS spectra of TiO2/CQDs, TiO2/BiOBr, and TiO2/CQDs/BiOBr composites were further employed, and the results were shown in Fig. 9. TiO2/BiOBr and TiO2/CQDs/BiOBr exhibited the signals of Bi, O, Br, Ti, and C, as displayed in Fig. 9 (a). The O1s high-resolutions scan (Fig. 9 (b)) in TiO2/CQDs and TiO2/CQDs/BiOBr can be deconvoluted into two peaks located at 529.5 and 531.8 eV, which could be attributed to Ti-O and O-H, respectively. The O1s peaks in TiO2/BiOBr were 529.8 and 531.8 eV, which belonged to Bi-O and O-H. As for the high-resolution spectra of Ti 2p (Fig. 9 (c)), two peaks located at 458.5 and 464.3 eV could be assigned to Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 in TiO2/CQDs, indicating that Ti in the sample was in the form of Ti4+ state. As for TiO2/BiOBr and TiO2/CQDs/BiOBr, there was a broad bump due to the partial overlap between Ti 2p1/2 at 464.3 eV and Bi 4d3/2 at 465.1 eV [32,33] caused by BiOBr introduce. The Br 3d (Fig. 9 (d)) peak located at 68.4 eV and Bi 4f exhibited in Fig. 9 (e), with two peaks located at 159.4 (Bi 4f7/2) and 164.5 eV (Bi 4f5/2), testifying the sufficient BiOBr loading in TiO2/BiOBr. However, the Bi 4f peaks shifted to higher position in TiO2/CQDs/BiOBr, demonstrating that the CQDs anchored on the BiOBr nanoflakes tightly and formed heterojunctions. Fig. 9 (f) exhibited the high resolution XPS spectra of the C 1s and the peaks at 284.7, 286.5, 288.4 eV could be attributed to C˭C, C-O, C-C in TiO2/CQDs, respectively, while C˭C (285.7 eV) and C-C (288.4 eV) in TiO2/CQDs/BiOBr, respectively [34].

Fig. 10 (a) showed the TEM and SAED images of TiO2/CQDs/BiOBr, where solid TiO2 microspheres and irregular BiOBr nanoparticles were observed. As shown in Fig. 10 (b), the interplanar distance with 0.357 nm and 0.352 nm, was assigned to the (101) plane of TiO2 and (011) plane of BiOBr, respectively. The dots marked in circle were speculated from carbon quantum dots with the spacing of 0.33 nm, corresponding to the (002) plane of carbon, demonstrating the effective formation of TiO2/CQDs/BiOBr heterojunctions.

Fig. 11 displayed the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of the TiO2, TiO2/BiOBr, and TiO2/CQDs/BiOBr, which could be assigned to type IV according to the classification of IUPAC. The part of hysteresis loop (0.4 <P/P0<1) existed due to the existence of large pores stacked in the samples, which suggested that CQDs or BiOBr aggregates on TiO2 surface [35]. From the specific surface area values shown in Table S1, TiO2/CQDs/BiOBr had the maximum specific surface area (55.2 m2/g), probably because the CQDs anchored on the surface of TiO2 spheres and supported the BiOBr nanoflakes simultaneously.

Fig. 12 (a) presented the UV–vis diffusive reflectance spectra of TiO2, TiO2/CQDs, TiO2/BiOBr, and TiO2/CQDs/BiOBr composites. After BiOBr was distributed on the surface of TiO2, a red-shifting absorption edges had been detected. The absorption curve of the ternary TiO2/CQDs/BiOBr showed the distinctly enhanced visible light absorption compared to the others. And it can be estimated the band gap energy from the plots of (αhv)0.5 versus hv. The band gap of TiO2, TiO2/BiOBr and TiO2/CQDs/BiOBr was calculated to be 3.05, 2.90 and 2.82 eV, respectively, indicating that the coupling of TiO2 with CQDs and BiOBr expanded the light-response range obviously.

Fig. 12 (b) showed the photocatalytic degradation plots by various catalysts prepared in our experiment, and self-degradation of MO was also carried out. The ternary heterostructured TiO2/CQDs/BiOBr realized about 85% of MO degradation during 180 min under simulated sunlight, which was twice as that of P25.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of enhanced photocatalytic performance by ternary TiO2/CQDs/BiOBr composites
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the experimental results above, the enhanced photocatalytic mechanism of TiO2/CQDs/BiOBr was proposed in Scheme 4. While TiO2 can hardly be excited by visible-light irradiation, BiOBr (2.60 eV) can absorb visible-light photons, producing electrons and holes, and can be regard as photo-sensitizer according to discussions above. The electrons on the conduction band (CB) of BiOBr could easily migrate to that of TiO2 due to the CB potential difference, while holes remained in the valence band (VB) of BiOBr. As a result, the electron-hole pairs could be separated effectively, leading a decreasing combination.

When CQDs was introduced in the systems of TiO2/CQDs/BiOBr, two aspects should be taken into consideration: On the one hand, CQDs can elevate the separation efficiency of photo-excited charges due to the outstanding conductivity. On the other hand, the CQDs located on the surface of TiO2 spheres or BiOBr nanoflakes could act as shallow traps, which could constrain electron-hole recombination. The oxidative holes in the VB of BiOBr can directly degrade of MO, and the reductive electrons in the CB of TiO2 can be transferred to the absorbed O2 to form O2·-, further oxidize MO to water and carbon dioxide. On the basis of above analysis, the synergistic effect between CQDs and TiO2/BiOBr resulted in excellent photocatalytic activity.

In summary, a series of novel TiO2/BiOX (X = Cl, Br, I) and TiO2/CQDs/BiOBr hybrid photocatalysts were successfully prepared through a facile hydrothermal method. Primarily, TiO2/BiOX heterostructures were fabricated through varying the halogen ions, and the effects of halogen on the composition and morphology were discussed. TiO2/BiOBr showed more preferable photocatalytic activities due to suitable band gap alignment and photo stability. Moreover, the effects of synthesis temperature and Bi/Ti ratio on the composition and structure were investigated, the results indicated TiO2/BiOBr with Bi/Ti= 10% prepared at 120 °C possessed the best photocatalytic performance. Finally, carbon quantum dots were introduced to accelerate electrons transfer at interface or prevent charges combination on the surface, in the synergistic system where BiOBr played the role of photo-sensitizer. Moreover, the facile synthesis process can provide a universal strategy for photocatalysts design in the field of waste-water treatment.
This work was mainly funded by the Nation Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, No. 61504073, 51272115).
The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jssc.2018.05.002.
